ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for banh mi; 'sandwich food product' is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM (Sandwich) used as the closest defensible category; no banh mi-specific code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for banh mi or Vietnamese sandwich; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 banh mi sandwich with side salad
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one complete banh mi sandwich with side salad, weighing approximately 450 g, comprising a crispy baguette filled with grilled pork belly, pickled vegetables, fresh herbs, and condiments, served alongside a fresh green salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork belly, raw (FDC ID 168375) and French bread/baguette (FDC ID 172687), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient data for banh mi-style sandwich components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for grilled pork belly banh mi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled pork belly (~40% by weight), French baguette (~25%), pickled vegetables (~15%), fresh salad (~10%), and condiments including mayonnaise, pâté, fish sauce (~10%). The input record's per_100g values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 12.22 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. HPB Singapore database consulted for sodium benchmarking of Vietnamese-style sandwiches sold in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled pork belly banh mi. GI estimated at 55 (low, upper boundary of low GI range ≤55) based on the analogous food 'white bread / baguette-based sandwich with protein and fat filling' from the University of Sydney GI Database. White bread GI is ~70; the substantial fat and protein content of pork belly, mayonnaise, and pâté slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response, moderating the effective GI to ~55. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 = low. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (54.99 g from 450 g × 12.22 g/100g) / 100 = 30.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, pâté, mayonnaise, and pickled vegetables
hypertension.guidance: At 333 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,500 mg per full 450 g serving), this dish approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, omit or minimise pâté, and limit the pickled vegetable portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate baguette with moderate glycaemic load (~30 per serving) partially offset by fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The baguette contributes refined carbohydrates with a low-to-medium GI (~55); the high fat and protein content from pork belly, mayonnaise, and pâté slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise, but the glycaemic load per full serving (~30) is substantial and individuals with type 2 diabetes should consider a half-portion baguette or substitute with a wholegrain roll.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and pâté combined with high sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork belly contributes approximately 4.0 g saturated fat per 100 g (18 g per serving), and the combination of pâté, mayonnaise, fish sauce, and soy sauce delivers a high sodium load (~1,500 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request leaner protein substitutions, and reduce high-sodium condiments to manage both atherogenic fat intake and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory red meat (pork belly) and processed meat (pâté) rich in arachidonic acid and saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork belly and pâté are both pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis due to high arachidonic acid (omega-6) and saturated fat content; individuals with RA should consider substituting pork belly with grilled chicken breast or tofu, omitting pâté, and monitoring for joint symptom exacerbation from tomato (nightshade) components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Pork Belly Banh Mi with Salad (Vietnamese: Bánh mì thịt ba chỉ nướng kèm salad; Chinese: 烤五花肉越南法棍配沙拉; Malay: Banh Mi Perut Babi Panggang dengan Salad) is a Vietnamese-origin sandwich dish served in Singapore. It consists of a crispy French baguette filled with chargrilled pork belly slices, liver pâté, mayonnaise, pickled daikon and carrot, fresh cucumber, coriander, and chilli, accompanied by a fresh green side salad. The pork belly is marinated and grilled to achieve a caramelised, slightly smoky exterior with tender, fatty interior layers. The baguette provides a light, airy crumb with a shatteringly crisp crust, a legacy of French colonial influence on Vietnamese cuisine. The flavour profile is a balance of savoury, umami-rich pork and pâté, tangy pickled vegetables, fresh herbal brightness from coriander, and mild heat from chilli. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 10.0 g fat (4.0 g saturated), and 12.2 g carbohydrate with 1.1 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 333 mg per 100 g (~1,500 mg per full 450 g serving) due to fish sauce, soy sauce, pâté, and mayonnaise. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat load; moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to refined carbohydrate baguette with a low GI (~55) and glycaemic load of ~30 per serving. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets (pork) and contains gluten, soy, and egg allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for grilled pork belly banh mi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled pork belly, French baguette, pickled vegetables, fresh salad, and condiments. The input record values are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread/baguette-based sandwich with protein and fat filling from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite dish with no direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (grilled pork belly FDC 168375, French baguette FDC 172687) and HPB Singapore benchmarks for Vietnamese-style sandwiches; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. 2. Monounsaturated fat (3.2 g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.5 g/100g) are estimated from USDA pork belly and mayonnaise component data; these are approximate. 3. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available from approved sources for this composite dish): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese sandwich preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as a single composite entry as of 2026. 4. Potassium estimated at 195 mg/100g from USDA pork belly and vegetable component data. 5. The dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal, kosher, or vegetarian/vegan diets. 6. Allergens include gluten (baguette), soy (soy sauce), and egg (mayonnaise). 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate classified as low GI (≤55 per University of Sydney classification); no direct GI measurement for banh mi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 8. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas rules. 9. This dish is Vietnamese cuisine served in Singapore. Mandatory Vietnamese local name is provided. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi transliterations/descriptive names are provided to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for a dish served in Singapore context. These are descriptive transliterations, not native-language dish names, as banh mi is not a Singaporean-origin dish. 10. gi_category corrected to 'low' (GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range ≤55 per University of Sydney GI Database classification). 11.
